summary:
Oliver Cromwell's Commonwealth and Protectorate (1649–1660 AD) was the brief English experiment with republican government following the [[event:2407|execution of Charles I]], during which Cromwell served as Lord Protector — a title carefully designed to be non-royal but whose holder exercised monarchical powers — governing a state that was officially a republic but functionally a military dictatorship dressed in Puritan religious language. Cromwell's decade of power was marked by extraordinary contradictions: a genuine religious liberator (he readmitted Jews to England for the first time since 1290; granted limited toleration to Protestant sects including Baptists and Quakers) who was simultaneously a brutal conqueror (the Irish campaign of 1649–1653 killed an estimated 200,000–600,000 through massacre, famine, and plague — a catastrophe etched in Irish memory as 'the curse of Cromwell').

The [[event:3501|conquest of Ireland]] was Cromwell's most consequential action: the massacre of Catholic garrisons at Drogheda and Wexford (1649) and the subsequent transplantation of Catholic Irish landowners ('to Hell or Connacht') and mass confiscation of Irish lands for Protestant settlement created a Protestant landowning class in Ireland whose socioeconomic dominance persisted for two centuries. The Navigation Acts (1651) required that English colonial goods be carried in English ships — a foundational mercantilist policy that built English maritime power and triggered the Anglo-Dutch Wars. Cromwell dissolved Parliament multiple times when it defied him, trying various constitutional arrangements (the Major-Generals military districts; the Instrument of Government) without finding a stable formula. His death in 1658 quickly led to the collapse of his system: his son Richard ('Tumbledown Dick') proved incapable, and Charles II was restored in 1660 — welcomed, paradoxically, by many Parliamentarians who had fought against his father.
